games.explanation: Rasmussen has quietly been one of the harder right-handers to square up this year, which is exactly why the market is happy to lay this price and exactly why it's wrong. The Red Sox stack lefty bats at the top of the order, and Rasmussen's split tells on him there, with a noticeably fatter xwOBA against left-handed hitters than righties. Duran and Abreu both punish right-handed pitching at a near-.340 clip, and Contreras is the kind of barrel-rate problem who turns a quality start into a bullpen game in a hurry. Throw in two near-equal Elo teams and you're getting plus money on the side our numbers actually prefer. +130 on the better lineup against the more exploitable arm is the entire pitch.

The Cubs are being priced like the obvious side here, and that's fair right up until you remember Imanaga is a fly-ball lefty walking into Coors with a crosswind blowing 14 mph. Two teams separated by a single Elo point shouldn't have a 25-cent gap on the moneyline, especially when the visiting starter gives up barrels to righties at a 13.5% clip and the Rockies have Goodman and Tovar waiting for exactly that pitch shape. Lorenzen isn't a star, but he's been steady enough to keep a game in altitude playable, and the park is doing 30% extra run-scoring work whether the market wants to admit it or not. Coors plus a vulnerable lefty plus a coin-flip matchup gets you +145, and that's the entire pitch.

picks.explanation: Rasmussen has quietly been one of the harder right-handers to square up this year, which is exactly why the market is happy to lay this price and exactly why it's wrong. The Red Sox stack lefty bats at the top of the order, and Rasmussen's split tells on him there, with a noticeably fatter xwOBA against left-handed hitters than righties. Duran and Abreu both punish right-handed pitching at a near-.340 clip, and Contreras is the kind of barrel-rate problem who turns a quality start into a bullpen game in a hurry. Throw in two near-equal Elo teams and you're getting plus money on the side our numbers actually prefer. +130 on the better lineup against the more exploitable arm is the entire pitch.

Tampa is the slightly better team here, but the price on a Rays runaway assumes Rasmussen does something he hasn't really done all year. He's been efficient, not dominant, and the lefty-heavy top of Boston's order is exactly the look that's chipped at him, with a .298 wOBA against from the left side hiding inside otherwise tidy numbers. Pair that with a projected one-run game and you've got the textbook setup for a backdoor cover, not a blowout. Duran, Abreu, and a Contreras bat that punishes everyone don't need to win the game, they just need to keep it within a run. At -170 on a coin flip with a built-in cushion, the math does the rest.

Nobody is going to confuse the Pirates with a contender, but a run and a half is a lot of cushion when the projected scoreline is a one-run game. Ohtani has been excellent, though the Pittsburgh lineup is built almost entirely of left-handed bats who run into him at his best, which sounds bad until you remember Lowe and Reynolds both carry an xwOBA north of .330 and have the kind of pull power that turns a wind-aided 90-degree night into a problem. The Dodgers are clearly the better team, but better teams win 6-4 just as often as they win 8-2, and the board is pricing this like a blowout. At +110, you're getting paid to bet that a sweaty Friday in a hitter's wind produces traffic, not a shutout.

The Cubs are being priced like the obvious side here, and that's fair right up until you remember Imanaga is a fly-ball lefty walking into Coors with a crosswind blowing 14 mph. Two teams separated by a single Elo point shouldn't have a 25-cent gap on the moneyline, especially when the visiting starter gives up barrels to righties at a 13.5% clip and the Rockies have Goodman and Tovar waiting for exactly that pitch shape. Lorenzen isn't a star, but he's been steady enough to keep a game in altitude playable, and the park is doing 30% extra run-scoring work whether the market wants to admit it or not. Coors plus a vulnerable lefty plus a coin-flip matchup gets you +145, and that's the entire pitch.
